name: Ogham
shortDescription: Ogham is an early medieval alphabet used primarily to write the early Irish language, and later for Old Welsh, Pictish and Latin. It also holds symbolic and divinatory significance within Celtic traditions.
description: Ogham is an Early Medieval alphabet primarily used to write the early Irish language (Old Irish), and later used for brief inscriptions in Old Welsh, Pictish, and Latin. It is sometimes called the 'Celtic Tree Alphabet' due to a modern association of its letters with trees, although this connection is considered secondary and likely a later development. The earliest examples of Ogham inscriptions are found on monumental stones, dating from the 4th to 6th centuries AD, primarily in Ireland and western Britain. The system consists of twenty primary letters, arranged in four groups (aicmí) of five letters each. Later, five additional letters were added (forfeda). Each letter is represented by a number of strokes placed along or across a central stemline. The direction of reading is typically bottom-to-top. While its primary function was practical, serving as a writing system, Ogham has also acquired a significant role in modern Celtic Neopaganism and other spiritual practices. In these contexts, the letters are often linked to trees, animals, and other natural symbols, and used for divination, meditation, and ritual work. This symbolic interpretation, though not definitively supported by historical evidence, has become a prominent aspect of the modern understanding of Ogham. The connection to druidry is often emphasized, although direct historical evidence linking Ogham to the ancient druids is limited, as druidic knowledge was primarily oral. However, the alphabet's association with trees and the natural world resonates with the known reverence for nature within druidic traditions. The association with trees is largely a modern interpretation, but it has become a powerful symbol within contemporary Celtic spirituality. The significance of ogham is not only linguistic but also extends into the realm of cultural identity and spiritual practice, making it a vital element in understanding Celtic heritage.

sources.title: Ogam Stones and the Earliest Irish Christians
sources.author: Patrick Sims-Williams
